context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on the State in development planning, and I’m kinda stuck on how to organize it. I think the State is important, but idk how to compare “Third World” vs “First World” State without oversimplifying.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair concern, but let's not get too caught up in structure just yet. Start with the substance: in your own words, what do you think makes the State different from, say, an NGO or a private company when it comes to development planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and raising a legitimate concern about oversimplification. Rather than giving organizational advice or scaffolding the structure for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about the substance first. Organization will follow from clarity of ideas. I'll push them to articulate what they already think about the State's role before worrying about structure.

exchanges.student: I think the State has actual authority to make rules, not just suggest stuff or fund projects. NGOs and firms can influence things, but the State can set laws and decide who gets resources, right?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. So what gives the State that rule-making power in the first place? Why do other actors accept it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified a key distinguishing feature of the State - its rule-making authority and legitimacy. This is a good starting point. I should affirm this direction and push them to deepen it by connecting it to the concept of legitimacy, which is a central theme in the course syllabus. I want them to think about what gives the State that authority, without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing legitimacy? Like ppl accept the State more bc it’s seen as the official authority, even if that gets messy in practice.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly. Now here's the harder question: do you think that legitimacy looks the same in a post-colonial developing country as it does in a wealthy industrialized one? What might complicate it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified legitimacy as the source of state authority, which is central to the course themes. I should affirm this and push them to apply it specifically to the assignment's core tension: does legitimacy look different in 'Third World' vs 'First World' states? This moves the conversation toward the prompt's key comparison without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: Probably not the same, bc post-colonial states can have weaker institutions or borders/history imposed from outside. I’m not fully sure if that means less legitimacy overall, or just a different kind.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really interesting distinction. Think about it this way: if a Third World state is pressured by the IMF or World Bank to adopt certain policies, who is actually setting the rules? What does that do to the state's role as you defined it earlier?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a thoughtful distinction between 'less legitimacy' and 'different legitimacy' - this is a genuinely sophisticated observation worth pushing further. I should encourage them to think about what external pressures (like the IMF, World Bank, colonial legacies) do to a state's ability to plan autonomously, which directly connects to the prompt's question about globalization's impact and the course theme about external actors.

exchanges.student: Then it feels like the State still enforces the rules, but maybe it didn’t fully choose them. So its role gets kinda constrained by external actors, which makes its authority look less autonomous.
exchanges.tutor: Good. So does globalization constrain all states equally, or is there something specific about how it affects Third World states differently from First World ones?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong connection between external pressure and constrained state autonomy - this directly addresses the globalization question in the prompt. This is a good moment to push them toward the Third World vs First World comparison more explicitly, since they've been building the conceptual tools to tackle it. I want them to think about whether First World states face similar constraints or if the dynamic is fundamentally different.

exchanges.student: I think it hits them differently bc poorer states depend more on aid, loans, trade access, and foreign investment. So globalization prob limits their policy choices more, while richer states have more leverage.
exchanges.tutor: You're building a strong argument. One piece of the prompt you haven't touched yet: how does the Third World state relate specifically to industry, the private sector, and social programs? Does it play the same role as a First World state there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now built a solid conceptual foundation covering: state authority, legitimacy, external constraints, and asymmetric globalization effects. We're about halfway through the planned exchanges and they have enough material to start writing. Rather than continuing to develop more ideas, I should push them toward synthesis and toward the remaining part of the prompt they haven't addressed yet: the specific role of the state in relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs. Then I should nudge them to start writing.

exchanges.student: I don’t think it’s the same, but idk how to say it clearly rn. Maybe Third World states are expected to do more at once—push industrial growth, manage weak markets, and still provide basic social programs?
exchanges.tutor: That's a useful observation. You now have ideas covering most of the prompt: state authority and legitimacy, the Third World vs First World distinction, globalization's unequal impact, and the state's multiple roles. I think it's time to stop generating ideas and start writing. What would your opening argument be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is onto something important about the developmental state concept - being expected to simultaneously drive industrialization, regulate weak markets, and provide social services. This is good enough to work with. We've now covered all the main components of the prompt. Rather than elaborating further, I should push them to start integrating all of this into their actual writing, since we've had about 6 exchanges and have enough material for a solid reflection piece.

exchanges.student: Maybe my opening claim is that the State is distinct bc it has legitimate rule-making power, but in development planning that power is uneven and shaped by history and globalization. Then I could say Third World states are often expected to do more than First World ones while having less autonomy.
exchanges.tutor: That's a compelling opening argument. Now stop planning and start drafting - write your first paragraph and share it here when you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong opening claim that ties together the key ideas we've developed. This is a good moment to encourage them to start writing rather than continue planning verbally. I should affirm the direction without polishing or expanding on it for them, and push them to actually draft it.

exchanges.student: Ok, here’s my rough first para: The State plays a central role in development planning because it has the authority to make and enforce rules that shape economic and social life. Unlike NGOs, donors, or private firms, the State has legitimacy as a governing authority, which allows it to define institutions, allocate resources, and set long-term priorities. However, the role of the State is not the same everywhere. In developing or “Third World” contexts, the State is often expected to manage economic growth, regulate markets, and provide social welfare under conditions shaped by colonial legacies, weak institutions, and pressure from global actors. Because of this, the State remains the main actor in planning, but its power is often more constrained and contested than that of many “First World” states.
exchanges.tutor: Good start - the argument is clear and well-structured. One thing to push on: you mention colonial legacies and weak institutions as constraints, but right now they're just listed. Can you say more about *how* those specifically affect the State's planning role? Beyond that, what's your next paragraph tackling?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid first paragraph that captures the main argument well. It covers legitimacy, the state's distinguishing features, and the Third World/First World contrast. I should give honest feedback without grading or polishing for them. The paragraph is good but could be pushed to be more specific - the colonial legacies and weak institutions point is stated but not really explained. I should point this out and ask them to think about it, then push them toward their next paragraph.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that makes sense — I kinda listed them without explaining. I think my next para should explain legitimacy and colonial history more clearly, like how imposed borders/institutions can make state planning less trusted or harder to carry out?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right direction. Stop planning and write it - then bring it back if you get stuck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track and has a clear sense of direction. We're getting close to the end of our planned exchanges and they seem capable of continuing independently. I should affirm the direction briefly and push them to draft rather than plan, since they have a tendency to discuss ideas before writing them. This is the moment to be less Socratic and more directive about getting words on the page.
